text: The minimally invasive spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy has become a routine procedure for management of benign or low-grade tumours of the body and tail of the pancreas. In this video, we present our method of temporarily occluding the splenic artery in a robotic spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy. This modification of the Kimura technique was developed at the Royal North Shore Hospital in Sydney, and is successfully being used for our last twelve robotic distal pancreatectomies. In this video, we demonstrate the use of a vascular clamp for temporary occlusion of the splenic artery, as opposed to the traditional method of applying vessel loops, therefore reducing arterial inflow to the spleen and indirectly reducing venous return. This not only maintains a relatively bloodless field, but also eases accurate dissection of a non-distended splenic vein.

Using the da Vinci robotic platform, the surgeon begins by dissecting the gastrocolic ligament along the greater curvature of the stomach to access the lesser sac. The stomach is retracted by hitching it to the abdominal wall. An ultrasound probe is used to locate the tumor on the pancreas and assess its proximity to the portal vein and the neck of the pancreas. Dissection proceeds along the inferior border of the pancreas with a medial to lateral approach, identifying the splenic vein and carefully dividing its venous tributaries from the pancreas.

A tear in a venous tributary is encountered, leading to rapid blood loss. A clip is initially applied, but a six-zero PDS suture is used to successfully tamponade the vein. The surgeon then divides the neck of the pancreas lateral to the portal vein using diathermy, due to the bulky nature of the pancreas. After division, the splenic artery is identified along the superior margin of the pancreas, and the pancreatic magna branch is clipped and divided. The splenic artery is gently mobilized off the pancreatic parenchyma.

A vascular clamp is introduced through a twelve millimeter port and applied across the splenic artery, providing immediate inflow occlusion. This technique minimizes blood loss and eases dissection towards the splenic hilum. During further dissection, bleeding from a small venous tributary is encountered, but the low flow due to clamping allows for easy identification and suture ligation with six-zero PDS.

The surgeon clears the tail of the pancreas from the splenic hilum, successfully dividing all venous tributaries and arterial branches. A four-zero proline suture is applied to control the proximal main pancreatic duct, followed by a continuous V-lock suture securing the pancreatic resection margin. A haemostatic agent is compacted onto the resection bed with fibrillar material. The vascular clamp is removed, and the omentum is used to cover the resection bed. The specimen is retrieved through the umbilical port before fascia closure.
